title: The Third Conchord

jokes.text: Well, needless to say, people are still talking about me in Barcelona. / I bet they are.
jokes.setup: Murray opens with a self-aggrandizing claim about his fame in Barcelona.
jokes.punchline: The dry, flat response 'I bet they are' signals total disbelief without argument — the listener is humoring him.
jokes.explanation: Classic FotC opener: Murray's delusional self-importance met with a deadpan non-challenge. The economy is excellent — one line of sycophantic praise, one line of disbelieving validation. Well-integrated into Murray's character but not especially original for this show.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Well, needless to say, people are still talking about me in Barcelona.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I bet they are.

jokes.text: Murray: 'Now I wasn't sure whether that was the magazine or the band. I've heard from both, I've got a no from the band...'
jokes.setup: Murray announces an interview with 'Rolling Stones' as a potential publicity win.
jokes.punchline: He reveals he genuinely contacted both the magazine AND the actual Rolling Stones band, and received a formal rejection from the band — implying he seriously pitched the world's most famous rock group an interview with the Conchords.
jokes.explanation: This is a quietly excellent joke. The setup sounds like routine manager talk, but the punchline reveals Murray's catastrophically misplaced ambition AND his follow-through — he actually reached out to the Stones and got a no. The casual delivery of 'I've got a no from the band' is perfect deadpan. High character integration; only Murray would both attempt this and report the rejection so matter-of-factly.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Murray, who's this guy?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I'm getting to that, Jemaine, all right? That's in the agenda under Todd.

jokes.text: Murray loops back to item one after being challenged, announces 'Item one: Todd. Yeah, he's the new band member.' Todd says 'Hi.' Murray immediately pivots: 'Right. Item two...'
jokes.setup: Bret and Jemaine demand to go back to item one. Murray obliges but treats it as already settled.
jokes.punchline: The 'item one' re-announcement is identical to the first time, Todd says 'Hi' again, and Murray immediately tries to move on — as if repeating the announcement answers the objection.
jokes.explanation: The callback structure is tight: the exact repetition reveals Murray views the announcement itself as sufficient justification. Todd's repeated 'Hi' is a nice beat. The escalation of Bret and Jemaine's exasperation against Murray's blithe indifference is classic FotC dynamic.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Now I wasn't sure whether that was the magazine or the band. I've heard from both, I've got a no from the band...

jokes.text: Todd plays a bongo solo that stops everyone in their tracks — the 'Whoa!' reaction and subsequent awed discussion of the bongo performance.
jokes.setup: Murray asks Todd to 'show them what you showed me yesterday at the dentist,' framing a bongo solo as some kind of miraculous event.
jokes.punchline: The reaction ('Whoa!') and Murray's follow-up ('Even better than yesterday. You could see all the details when your fingers bounce off the skins') treat a competent bongo solo like a transcendent artistic experience.
jokes.explanation: The visual gag of Todd playing bongos to stunned silence works well partly because bongos are inherently silly and partly because the awe is disproportionate. Murray's observation about 'fingers bouncing off the skins' is classic clueless-expert commentary. Works better on rewatch when you know the bongo joke is sustained.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Do you want me to go back to item one?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Yeah, go back to item one.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Item one: Todd. Yeah, he's the new band member.

dialogue.speaker: Todd
dialogue.line: Hi.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Right. Item two...

jokes.text: Even better than yesterday. You could see all the details when your fingers bounce off the skins. Pigskin, is it? Really good.
jokes.setup: Murray has been a dental patient receiving nitrous oxide when he hired Todd.
jokes.punchline: His post-nitrous music criticism — specific, detailed, knowledgeable — delivered with complete sincerity. 'Pigskin, is it? Really good.' is peak Murray: the wrong context, the right authority.
jokes.explanation: Murray's earnest connoisseurship applied to bongos heard under nitrous oxide is delightful. 'Pigskin, is it?' as a serious material inquiry is a very specific Murray touch.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Whoa!

jokes.text: Todd: 'I'll just tell you straight up that all I'm really interested in is kicking some grooves, banging some tunes, I don't know, just basically getting it on.' Murray writes down 'Getting down.'
jokes.setup: Todd delivers a stream of vague musician-bro philosophy about his artistic goals.
jokes.punchline: Murray, treating this like actionable meeting minutes, transcribes it as simply 'Getting down.' — the bureaucratic reduction of Todd's posturing.
jokes.explanation: Two jokes in one: Todd's musician-cliché speak is gently lampooned, and Murray's earnest minute-taking of it is perfectly in character. The slight error ('Getting down' vs. 'Getting it on') adds a layer — Murray is both earnest and inattentive.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Even better than yesterday. You could see all the details when your fingers bounce off the skins. Pigskin, is it?

jokes.text: Murray claims adding Todd was a good idea like his past managerial advice: 'Like that time I saw you for the first time onstage. I said "Face the front," and you did. We've never looked back since, have we?'
jokes.setup: Murray defends his decision-making record, asking when he's ever had a good idea.
jokes.punchline: His single cited example of managerial genius is simply telling them to face the audience — the most basic possible stage direction — delivered with complete pride.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's stronger jokes. Murray's self-assessment as a valuable manager is undermined instantly: his proudest achievement is 'face the front.' The phrase 'We've never looked back since' is inadvertently ironic and quotable. Perfect character integration — this is exactly how Murray sees himself. Jemaine's deadpan 'Still not sure about that' seals it.

dialogue.speaker: Todd
dialogue.line: I'll just tell you straight up that all I'm really interested in is kicking some grooves, banging some tunes, I don't know, just basically getting it on.

jokes.text: It's too late, Jemaine. I think I promised him. I'd just been given nitrous oxide. I was at the dentist. I didn't know what I was talking about. I think I'm going to his brother's wedding.
jokes.setup: Jemaine tells Murray to fire Todd. Murray explains why he can't.
jokes.punchline: The escalating confessions: he promised while on nitrous oxide... and now he's going to Todd's brother's wedding. Each revelation is worse than the last. The wedding commitment, delivered as an afterthought, is the peak.
jokes.explanation: Classic escalation structure: nitrous oxide → can't fire him → I promised → I'm going to his brother's wedding. The wedding is the perfect capper because it implies a depth of social commitment that goes far beyond a professional decision made while impaired. The structure earns its 8.5 — each beat lands independently and together they build beautifully.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Come on, Todd, we're having a private band meeting.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: No, I mean without Todd.

jokes.text: Murray: 'I had to. No one else was gonna do it. You guys weren't gonna come up with that idea, were you?' / Murray: 'I'm on a call here with the Secretary of State. Could you... how much longer are you gonna be?' / 'Oh, not much longer, Greg. Sorry.'
jokes.setup: Murray is arguing passionately about band decisions mid-meeting.
jokes.punchline: Someone off-screen ('Greg') interrupts — Murray is apparently on a call with someone at the consulate he calls 'the Secretary of State,' and his total reversal to deferential 'Oh, not much longer, Greg. Sorry' undercuts his authority instantly.
jokes.explanation: The joke works on two levels: Murray's grandiose claim that no one else could have thought of adding a band member is punctured by having to apologize to his boss mid-speech. The detail that the consulate colleague is called 'Greg' while being referred to as 'Secretary of State' adds absurdist scale to a mundane moment.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Sometimes I have good ideas.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: When?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Like that time I saw you for the first time onstage. I said 'Face the front,' and you did.

jokes.text: Murray: 'Like the song.' / Jemaine: 'Which song?' / Murray: 'The John Lennon one, you know.' / Jemaine: 'Give Peace a Chance.' / Murray: 'Give Pete a Chance.'
jokes.setup: Murray wants everyone to 'give Todd a chance' and reaches for a cultural reference to make his point.
jokes.punchline: He misremembers 'Give Peace a Chance' as 'Give Pete a Chance,' which is both stupid and accidentally funnier — Todd's name being Pete-adjacent makes it feel like a Freudian slip.
jokes.explanation: A clean, economic little joke. Murray's cultural incompetence is consistent, and 'Give Pete a Chance' is a genuinely funny malapropism. The no-correction from anyone in the room except a flat 'Peace' makes it land with proper deadpan weight.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I'd just been given nitrous oxide. I was at the dentist. I didn't know what I was talking about. I think I'm going to his brother's wedding.

jokes.text: After Todd plays, Murray says: 'But, um, not every song should end with a bongo solo.' Todd: 'Sure, no problem.'
jokes.setup: Todd has just played what is clearly an extended bongo solo to end the song.
jokes.punchline: Murray's diplomatic critique is the mildest possible objection to what was presumably an egregious solo, and Todd's instant breezy agreement implies he'll simply ignore it.
jokes.explanation: Functional setup for the running bongo-solo gag. Sets up Todd's character as someone who agrees and then ignores all feedback, but doesn't fully land on its own — it's more scaffolding than punchline.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You're like a proper band. You're like The Policemen.

jokes.text: Todd suggests a song where Bret and Jemaine put their guitars down and bark like dogs ('Arf, arf!') while he plays bongos.
jokes.setup: Todd is excited about his artistic vision for the band's new direction.
jokes.punchline: His big creative idea is that the two actual musicians — who play guitar and bass — should act as background dogs while he solos.
jokes.explanation: Efficient absurdist comedy that seeds the entire 'Doggy Bounce' arc. The specificity ('bad dogs') and Todd's absolute confidence are key. Sets up a rich vein of comedy for later in the episode. The rewatch value is high because you know where this goes.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I'm on a... call here with the Secretary of State. Could you... how much longer are you gonna be?

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'I dance when I'm angry.' / Todd: 'When you're angry? Who dances when they're angry?' / Jemaine: 'I guess that's about it.'
jokes.setup: Todd challenges Jemaine's claim that he likes dancing, and Jemaine lists contexts in which he dances.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine includes 'when I'm angry' as a dance context (already absurd), gets called out on it, and then collapses the whole defence with 'I guess that's about it' — abandoning his remaining examples.
jokes.explanation: A tight little joke with a lovely structure: setup-absurdist detail-challenge-deflation. 'I guess that's about it' is a perfectly Jemaine response — he doesn't defend himself, he just gives up. High character integration; the capitulation is funnier than any comeback would be.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Give Peace a Chance.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Give Pete a Chance.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Peace.

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'The thing is, uh, we've been practicing for 56 minutes and... that's how long we practice for.'
jokes.setup: Todd is excitedly pushing for one more song run-through.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's excuse for stopping is the exact duration: they've reached their predetermined practice time limit of 56 minutes — a weirdly specific and deliberately non-round number that implies both laziness and overly rigid scheduling.
jokes.explanation: '56 minutes' is the entire joke and it earns its laugh. The specificity is perfect — it's not an hour, it's 56. It implies a rule was established for bad reasons and is being followed with total rigidity. Pure character comedy: two musicians so fundamentally unmotivated they have a capped practice time, and Jemaine states it without irony or apology.

dialogue.speaker: Todd
dialogue.line: Can we try a song that I wrote real quick?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No.

jokes.text: Mel: 'Whoa-ho! What are the chances? We're always bumping into each other.' / Bret: 'About one in one.'
jokes.setup: Mel, the obsessive fan, acts surprised to run into the band she stalks.
jokes.punchline: Bret's flat mathematical correction — 'About one in one' — acknowledges the stalking without accusing her of it.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. 'About one in one' is four words that perfectly encapsulate Mel's stalker status, Bret's passive-aggressive awareness of it, and his inability to directly confront it. Exceptional economy. Highly quotable and replayable. The structure is airtight: the more Mel's surprise is performed, the funnier the mathematical correction.

dialogue.speaker: Todd
dialogue.line: All you guys'll do, you'll put your guitars and bass down and you just go, 'Arf arf!' In the background, like dogs. Like bad dogs.

jokes.text: Todd introduces himself to Mel as 'the third Conchord,' then flirts aggressively. Murray told him about Mel but 'neglected to tell me what a hottie you are.'
jokes.setup: Todd meets the band's superfan Mel for the first time.
jokes.punchline: His instant sleazy pivot to hitting on her ('neglected to tell me what a hottie you are') is both a character-establishing moment and a commentary on the fact that Murray described her as a 'maniac' but apparently also hyped her looks.
jokes.explanation: Functional character introduction for Todd's bro-ish confidence, but the joke itself is fairly generic sleazy-guy comedy. Works as setup for later Todd-Mel jokes rather than as a standalone.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I dance when I'm angry.

dialogue.speaker: Todd
dialogue.line: When you're angry? Who dances when they're angry?

jokes.text: Todd: 'Looks like the race is on. Gentlemen, start your engines. Vroom!' / Bret and Jemaine awkwardly attempt 'Vroom!' back.
jokes.setup: Todd declares competitive romantic interest in Mel in front of Bret and Jemaine.
jokes.punchline: His 'vroom!' car-race metaphor is so aggressively confident and performative that it demands participation — and the two Conchords' halfhearted 'Vroom!' echoes are perfectly embarrassing.
jokes.explanation: The cringe is in Bret and Jemaine feeling socially obligated to 'vroom' back — their compliance despite obvious reluctance is the joke. Todd's unearned confidence reading a non-existent competition is a good character beat.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Whoa-ho! What are the chances? We're always bumping into each other.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: About one in one.

jokes.text: Mel: 'What do you play, Todd?' / Todd: 'I'll give you a clue.' / Mel: 'The bongos.' (immediately, before he gives any clue)
jokes.setup: Todd theatrically offers to give Mel a clue about his instrument.
jokes.punchline: Mel immediately names it before he can say anything, deflating his performance entirely.
jokes.explanation: Tight misdirection gag — Todd's theatrical buildup is instantly punctured by Mel's blunt answer. The economy is excellent. It also subtly suggests bongos are so obvious as to require no clue whatsoever.

dialogue.speaker: Todd
dialogue.line: Actually, he, um, neglected to tell me what a hottie you are.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Stop it.

dialogue.speaker: Todd
dialogue.line: Looks like the race is on. Gentlemen, start your engines. Vroom! Vroom!

jokes.text: Mel on bongos: 'Oh! Rhythmic.' / Todd: 'Yeah, tribal.' / Mel: 'Powerful.' / Todd: 'Very physical, if you know what I mean.' / Mel: 'Oh, I know what you mean, Todd.'
jokes.setup: Todd and Mel escalate an increasingly loaded exchange about the bongos.
jokes.punchline: Both are transparently flirting under the guise of discussing an instrument, culminating in the double-entendre 'very physical, if you know what I mean' / 'Oh, I know what you mean, Todd' — which is both cringeworthy and alarming given Mel is married.
jokes.explanation: Competent cringe comedy. The escalating double-entendres are functional but not especially original — instrument-as-metaphor flirting is familiar territory. The joke is more about Mel's instant susceptibility to Todd than anything particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: What do you play, Todd?

dialogue.speaker: Todd
dialogue.line: I'll give you a clue.

dialogue.speaker: Todd
dialogue.line: The bongos.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Oh! Rhythmic.

dialogue.speaker: Todd
dialogue.line: Yeah, tribal.

dialogue.speaker: Todd
dialogue.line: Powerful. Very physical, if you know what I mean.

jokes.text: Mel's parting line to Todd: 'Sweet dreams, baby.'
jokes.setup: Mel is saying goodbye to the group after a brief encounter.
jokes.punchline: She reserves 'Sweet dreams, baby' exclusively for Todd — an absurdly intimate farewell to someone she met two minutes ago, completely ignoring the bandmates she has obsessively followed for apparently years.
jokes.explanation: Three words and a perfect punch. The joke lands because of what's not said — the implicit slight to Bret and Jemaine whom she is obsessed with. The intimacy of 'baby' to a stranger is also darkly funny given Mel's character.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: She's a maniac.

dialogue.speaker: Todd
dialogue.line: In the sack? Really?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No. What?

jokes.text: Todd: 'So what's she like? She's a maniac. In the sack? Really?' / Jemaine: 'No. What?'
jokes.setup: Todd asks what Mel is like; Murray says she's a maniac.
jokes.punchline: Todd instantly interprets 'maniac' sexually, and when he's corrected, his 'Really?' implies disappointment that she isn't.
jokes.explanation: Clean, quick joke. Works mostly through Todd's character — his mind immediately goes there — and Jemaine's horrified 'No. What?' is a good straight-man beat. Jemaine's 'What?' implies 'why would you even ask that' which is funnier than a simple denial.

dialogue.speaker: Todd
dialogue.line: Oh, I thought you had a threesome with her.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No, she's just a fan. Never had a threesome with her.

jokes.text: Todd: 'Oh, I thought you had a threesome with her.' / Jemaine: 'No, she's just a fan. Never had a threesome with her.'
jokes.setup: Todd continues pressing about the nature of the relationship with Mel.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's denial includes the oddly specific qualifier 'Never had a threesome with her' — the phrasing implies threesomes are a normal enough occurrence that this particular one simply hasn't happened.
jokes.explanation: The humor is in Jemaine's oddly categorical denial — it's not 'I've never had a threesome' but 'Never had a threesome with her,' which implies nothing about other threesomes. The overprecision is the joke. Good deadpan from Jemaine.

dialogue.speaker: Todd
dialogue.line: Who cares? I'm a bongo player in a band. She's hot to trot. I'm gonna make it happen. I'm gonna give it to her.

jokes.text: Todd: 'Well, if you two homo sapiens aren't gonna make the move, guess what? I'm gonna.' / 'Who cares? I'm a bongo player in a band. She's hot to trot.'
jokes.setup: Todd declares his intent to pursue Mel romantically, dismissing the fact she's married.
jokes.punchline: 'I'm a bongo player in a band' is offered as a complete and sufficient justification for why a married woman should leave her husband — a delusion of bongo-based rock-star magnetism.
jokes.explanation: The joke is entirely in 'I'm a bongo player in a band' as a mic-drop argument. Bongos are inherently not a power instrument, and 'in a band' is redundant — yet Todd delivers it with total swagger. Highly quotable line that encapsulates his character.

dialogue.speaker: Todd
dialogue.line: Well, if you two homo sapiens aren't gonna make the move, guess what? I'm gonna.

jokes.text: Well, if you two homo sapiens aren't gonna make the move, guess what? I'm gonna. I'm definitely gonna do it with her.
jokes.setup: Todd declares his intention to pursue Mel.
jokes.punchline: 'You two homo sapiens' — Todd uses the scientific term for humans as if it's an insult or a distinguishing category. It implies he sees himself as somehow beyond homo sapien. The phrase is so unexpected it becomes funny.
jokes.explanation: 'Homo sapiens' as an insult/distinction is a very specific and original choice that suggests Todd thinks of himself as some evolved being beyond normal human classification. Weird, unexpected, and very funny in a deadpan delivery.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: He's the Pied Piper of cool.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Pied Piper was a good musician, wasn't he?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: He was cool.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: He wasn't cool.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yes, he was.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: He took all those kids into a cave.

jokes.text: Oh no, she's married. / Who cares? I'm a bongo player in a band.
jokes.setup: Jemaine points out that Mel is married as a reason Todd shouldn't pursue her.
jokes.punchline: Todd's counterargument is not that the marriage is irrelevant — it's that his status as 'a bongo player in a band' supersedes marital considerations. This is the most delusional form of rock star logic applied to being a bongo player in an extremely small band.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's standout jokes. 'Who cares? I'm a bongo player in a band' is superb because it applies rock-god logic to the most unglamorous possible position in the most unsuccessful possible band. The gap between the grandiosity of the claim and the reality is enormous, and Todd is completely unaware of it. Highly quotable, great economy, strong character integration. Justifies 8+ score.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: No, before that phase, when just the rats followed him...

jokes.text: Todd's repeated 'Am I right? Am I right? / Possibly. / Possibly? I'm right.'
jokes.setup: Todd seeks validation from Bret and Jemaine for his plan to pursue Mel.
jokes.punchline: The Conchords' reluctant 'Possibly' and Todd's indignant 'Possibly? I'm right' — he demands agreement and then declares himself correct regardless.
jokes.explanation: 'Am I right?' becomes Todd's verbal tic throughout the episode. The first instance already shows his need for affirmation and his unwillingness to accept anything less than full agreement. 'Possibly? I'm right.' is the key line — concise and revealing.

dialogue.line: Your solos were too long?
No no no, the band name is too long.
What is it? Flight of the Con... Con... Conchords...
Blah blah blah. Too long, too boring.

jokes.text: Bret: 'He's not cool like us. We're cool.' / Murray: 'He's way cool! He's cooler than both of you put together.'
jokes.setup: Bret and Jemaine argue Todd should be fired because 'he's not cool.'
jokes.punchline: Murray immediately and enthusiastically disagrees — in the world of the show, the two cooler-than-thou Kiwis are obviously not cool, and Murray calling Todd 'cooler than both of you put together' is both true and devastating.
jokes.explanation: Functional comedy of self-delusion — Bret and Jemaine genuinely believe they're cool. Works because the audience knows they're not. Murray's enthusiastic reversal is the punchline.

dialogue.speaker: Todd
dialogue.line: Get ready... The Crazy Dogggz. Three Gs and a Z.

jokes.text: Murray: 'He's like the Pied Piper of cool.' / Jemaine: 'Pied Piper was a good musician, wasn't he?' / Murray: 'He was cool.' / Jemaine: 'He wasn't cool. He took all those kids into a cave.'
jokes.setup: Murray compares Todd to the Pied Piper.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine undermines the metaphor by accurately recalling the dark conclusion of the Pied Piper myth — luring children into a cave — delivered as a flat factual counterpoint to 'he was cool.'
jokes.explanation: Excellent deployment of the dark-subversive angle without being tagged as such — the Pied Piper's child-abduction aspect is delivered as a perfectly reasonable argument against his coolness. 'He took all those kids into a cave' as a rebuttal to 'he was cool' is genuinely sharp writing. The pre-defence ('No, before that phase, when just the rats followed him') is also funny as an attempted rehabilitation of the Piper's cool.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You're too easily offended.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I can't believe you just said that.

jokes.text: Murray: 'No, before that phase, when just the rats followed him... the Pied Piper's not cool and Todd's not cool.'
jokes.setup: Murray tries to salvage the Pied Piper comparison by limiting it to the rat phase.
jokes.punchline: Abandoning his own metaphor mid-defence, Murray ends with a flat 'the Pied Piper's not cool and Todd's not cool' — completely reversing his earlier position because the analogy collapsed.
jokes.explanation: The callback escalation is good — Murray's attempted rescue of his own metaphor by proposing a 'before that phase' distinction is almost philosophical, and his sudden pivot to agreeing he's not cool is a satisfying collapse. The logic of 'just the rats followed him' as the cool phase is also funny in its absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I told him he was fired, he pushed me.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Then he said that you should be fired because you're the one holding us back.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Well, I hit him.

jokes.text: Todd objects to the band name being too long. 'What is it? Flight of the Con...Con...Conchords...blah blah blah.'
jokes.setup: Todd identifies a 'problem' with the gig.
jokes.punchline: The problem is the show's own title, which Todd dismisses with 'blah blah blah' — meta commentary on the band name being unwieldy, delivered by the character whose entire role is to be a disruptive outsider.
jokes.explanation: Lightly meta — Todd's contempt for the band name that titles the show is an implicit dig at artsy indie naming conventions. The trailing off into 'blah blah blah' after struggling to get through 'Conchords' is a nice visual/verbal gag.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Well, you see, while I had him in a headlock, he sort of explained why he thought you shouldn't be in the band. And after a while it kind of made sense.

jokes.text: Todd unveils the new band name: 'The Crazy Dogggz. Three Gs and a Z.' / Murray: 'I like it. You guys are crazy, you're like dogs.'
jokes.setup: Todd has been building up to his genius rebranding solution.
jokes.punchline: The name is 'Crazy Dogggz' with the spelling specified ('Three Gs and a Z'), and Murray's endorsement — 'You guys are crazy, you're like dogs' — accepts this logic completely without irony.
jokes.explanation: 'Three Gs and a Z' is the punchline within the punchline — the absurdity of specifying the typography of an already terrible name, as if that's the creative masterstroke. Murray's earnest explanation of the name's logic ('you're like dogs') is perfectly clueless. This joke rewards the whole episode's dog motif setup.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: You were only over there for like 20 seconds.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yeah, after about 20 seconds it kind of made sense.

jokes.text: You have the too long part right, but it's not my solo. It's the band name. What is it? Flight of the Con... Con... Conchords... Blah blah blah. Too long, too boring.
jokes.setup: Todd acknowledges Jemaine was partly right — something IS too long — but redirects to the band name.
jokes.punchline: 'Blah blah blah' as Todd's rendition of 'Conchords' — he can't even finish saying the band's name because it's too tedious. This is a remarkable piece of disrespect from a man who has been in the band for approximately three days.
jokes.explanation: The 'blah blah blah' dismissal of the band's actual name is a great piece of Todd obliviousness. The self-awareness gap is enormous — he's in this band and already considers its name too boring to say.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: It's easy for me to fire you, Bret, because you're my friend.

jokes.text: Bret: 'Okay, I'm just gonna go over there and say, "Dear Todd, thank you very much, but you can't be in the band anymore."' / Jemaine: 'Make sure you say "Dear Todd."'
jokes.setup: Bret psychs himself up to fire Todd.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's only contribution to the pep talk is to ensure Bret uses the formal salutation 'Dear Todd' — prioritizing politeness over effectiveness in a way that's both very New Zealand and very Jemaine.
jokes.explanation: Jemaine's 'Make sure you say Dear Todd' is a perfect encapsulation of how this show treats confrontation — with elaborate politeness rituals that undermine any actual confrontation. The formal letter-writing formality applied to an interpersonal firing is very FotC.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Flup!

jokes.text: Bret tells Jemaine he fired Todd; then reveals that while he had Todd in a headlock, Todd 'explained why he thought you shouldn't be in the band... after about 20 seconds it kind of made sense.'
jokes.setup: Bret reports back from attempting to fire Todd, claiming violence occurred.
jokes.punchline: The reveal that Bret, mid-headlock, was talked around to Todd's position in twenty seconds — and then fired Jemaine instead — is a perfect inversion of the mission.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's standout comedic sequences. The escalation is exquisite: Bret hit him (shock), then the reveal that Todd talked him around in 20 seconds while in a headlock (absurd), then Bret fires Jemaine instead of Todd (complete inversion). 'It's easy for me to fire you, Bret, because you're my friend' is a brilliant punchline that reframes friendship as a liability. Structural perfection.

dialogue.speaker: Todd
dialogue.line: More enthusiasm this time, all right?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Todd, how about you get down on all fours and wag your tail like you just can't fail and I'll just play bass, huh?

jokes.text: Bret: 'But you can't do that...' / Jemaine: 'Listen, we'll talk about it later. I've gotta go to band practice now. Todd's very strict.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine has just fired Bret and is deflecting.
jokes.punchline: 'Todd's very strict' — Jemaine has completely realigned with the man he sent Bret to fire, and now cites Todd's authority with reverence.
jokes.explanation: The speed of Jemaine's capitulation to Todd's authority is the joke. 'Todd's very strict' as a reason to rush off, delivered with total sincerity, is a great tag to the headlock scene. Character-integrated: Jemaine abandons his friend with polite efficiency.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I'm not Gloria Estefan, all right?

jokes.text: Flup!
jokes.setup: Bret has just been fired by his best friend and bandmate.
jokes.punchline: 'Flup!' — a single invented exclamation that is Bret's entire emotional response to being fired. It's not a real word, but it perfectly communicates a kind of dejected, resigned surprise. The lack of a proper reaction IS the reaction.
jokes.explanation: One word — one invented, non-word — does everything. 'Flup' is perhaps the most economical joke in the episode. The sound itself is funny: not 'Damn,' not 'What?', not a swear word, just 'Flup.' It's entirely Bret — gentle, slightly pathetic, completely unexpected. Economy score of 10 is warranted because there is literally nothing more compressed than this. Highly quotable in context. Justifies 8+.

dialogue.speaker: Todd
dialogue.line: It's not about the music. You know what it's about? # The pussy! #

jokes.text: Jemaine in band practice: 'Todd, how about you get down on all fours and wag your tail like you just can't fail and I'll just play bass, huh?'
jokes.setup: Todd is insisting everyone perform 'The Doggy Bounce' with full choreography.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine sarcastically inverts the roles — offering to let Todd do the dog performance while he (the actual musician) plays bass — highlighting the absurdity of what Todd is actually asking of them.
jokes.explanation: The rhyme 'wag your tail like you just can't fail' is an unexpectedly poetic construction for a sarcastic suggestion — very Jemaine. The inversion (bongo player does the dog act, guitarist plays normally) is satisfying given the earlier setup.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Do you guys need to borrow my amp?

dialogue.speaker: Todd
dialogue.line: Bret, you're pathetic, man. We don't need a roadie, okay?

jokes.text: Todd, how about you get down on all fours and wag your tail like you just can't fail and I'll just play bass, huh?
jokes.setup: Todd is directing the band through 'The Doggy Bounce,' telling them to bark and bounce. Jemaine negotiates.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's counter-proposal: Todd does all the dog performance while Jemaine just plays bass like a normal musician. The rhyme ('wag your tail like you just can't fail') suggests Jemaine has been composing this offer in his head, making it funnier that it's so carefully worded.
jokes.explanation: The internal rhyme ('fail'/'tail') in Jemaine's protest suggests a man who has been sitting with his frustration and finding poetic ways to express it. Character-integrated and well-constructed.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: So is a guitar or a keyboard?

dialogue.speaker: Demetri
dialogue.line: Exactly. It's the best of both worlds.

jokes.text: Todd stops practice: 'Bret, what are you doing?' / Bret: 'I'm not Gloria Estefan, all right?'
jokes.setup: Bret has apparently been doing background percussion or movement Todd didn't ask for.
jokes.punchline: Bret's protest — 'I'm not Gloria Estefan' — is a non-sequitur that somehow perfectly encapsulates his objection to being made to perform choreography. The specificity of Gloria Estefan (known for energetic Latin pop dance) is funnier than any generic 'I don't dance' protest.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most quotable lines. 'I'm not Gloria Estefan' is a perfect absurdist specificity — why Gloria Estefan? The inexplicability is the point. It's a completely Bret non-sequitur that communicates everything about his dignity objections to the Doggy Bounce while being entirely unexpected. Exceptional economy and originality.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: No, don't like it. Ugh, no. Weird.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Oh, that bit's good.

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'Todd, I don't like this kind of music.' / Todd: 'It's not about the music. You know what it's about? [sings] The pussy!'
jokes.setup: Jemaine makes a principled artistic objection to 'The Doggy Bounce.'
jokes.punchline: Todd dismisses the entire concept of music as the point of music, then delivers his real motive — attracting women — as an actual sung musical phrase.
jokes.explanation: The joke has a double punch: first, Todd's dismissal of music's importance in a band, then the irony of singing his non-musical motivation. The fact that he SINGS 'the pussy' — using the musical form to announce that music is not the point — is genuinely clever. Jemaine's 'obviously you're right, yeah' is a perfect deadpan capitulation.

dialogue.speaker: Demetri
dialogue.line: Oh, here we go. The Fonzies have arrived.

jokes.text: Bret offers to carry Todd's bongos and then Jemaine's guitar as a roadie. 'No one's touching my bongos. You know that.' / Jemaine: 'Well, I don't really need you to, Bret.'
jokes.setup: Bret, newly fired from the band, desperately tries to remain useful.
jokes.punchline: Todd's protective 'No one's touching my bongos. You know that' is both possessive and weirdly intimate — delivered like a statement of absolute law. Jemaine's gentler but equally deflating rejection completes the humiliation.
jokes.explanation: The cringe of Bret's eager roadie offer is well-played, and 'No one's touching my bongos. You know that.' is delivered with comical gravity. The 'You know that' implies a rule Bret could not possibly know since they've just met — it's like a long-established law being cited for the first time.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: It's called the Original Flight of the Conchords. You guys can be called Flight of the Condors, or whatever.

jokes.text: Am I right? / Yeah, obviously you're right, yeah. / So why do you care about the music?
jokes.setup: After declaring the purpose of the music, Todd demands agreement.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's 'Yeah, obviously you're right, yeah' — defeated, deflated agreement — followed by Todd turning it back: 'So why do you care about the music?' The logic trap is complete. Jemaine has argued himself into Todd's corner.
jokes.explanation: The 'am I right?' running gag pays off here because Jemaine concedes the point and immediately gets trapped by his own concession. Good structural callback.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: We shouldn't even be arguing in front of the map. It's not right.

jokes.text: Do you need someone to carry your guitar, Jemaine? / Well, I don't really need you to, Bret. / Okay. / I get it. / Not needed.
jokes.setup: Bret, unable to help Todd, turns to Jemaine with the same offer.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's gentle but firm 'I don't really need you to' — followed by Bret's self-processing ('Okay. I get it. Not needed.') — is quietly devastating. Bret talking himself through being rejected, step by step, is the cringe comedy.
jokes.explanation: 'Okay. I get it. Not needed.' is a perfect small Bret moment — three short sentences that process rejection in real time, each one slightly more self-aware than the last. It's not trying to be a big joke, but it lands as one of the episode's most human moments. The restraint is the craft.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: What do you play, the leather jacket?

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'It's not ready.' / Todd: 'It's totally ready.' / Jemaine: 'It's not ready, Todd.' [cut] Todd: 'All right, you're right. Let's get it perfect.' / Jemaine: 'Yeah, let's get it perfect and then we'll maybe do it another time.' / Todd: 'Yeah, am I right?' / Jemaine: 'Yes.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine and Todd argue about whether 'The Doggy Bounce' is ready to perform.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine wins the argument but then articulates the real goal — 'let's get it perfect and then we'll maybe do it another time' — revealing his only aim is to delay performing it indefinitely.
jokes.explanation: 'Let's get it perfect and then we'll maybe do it another time' perfectly captures Jemaine's passive-aggressive evasiveness. He's not saying no — he's engineering infinite postponement. Todd agreeing ('am I right?') without realizing he's been defeated is the cherry on top.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: We shouldn't even be arguing in front of the map.

jokes.text: Murray: 'Two of you? But that's going backwards. I thought we had a proper three-person band.'
jokes.setup: Bret has formed a new two-person band with Demetri after being fired.
jokes.punchline: Murray's complaint is that two members is 'going backwards' — not because Bret was fired or because there's been a schism, but because the headcount is suboptimal.
jokes.explanation: Murray's band-accounting mentality reduces the entire interpersonal drama to a headcount problem. Functional character comedy, not particularly sharp, but consistent with Murray's managerial worldview.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Well, I was trying to fire Todd but he did a sad face at me.

jokes.text: Bret introduces Demetri's instrument: 'It's a guitboard.' / Demetri: 'No, man, keytar. Keytar.' / Murray: 'Yeah, he took the wrong parts of the words.'
jokes.setup: Demetri plays a keytar; Bret describes it.
jokes.punchline: Bret names it 'guitboard' — taking the wrong half of each component word — and Murray's analysis ('he took the wrong parts of the words') is delivered as sober linguistic criticism.
jokes.explanation: 'Guitboard' is a perfect Bret malfunction — he gets the logic right (portmanteau of guitar + keyboard) but inverts both halves. Murray's explanation is delivered with the matter-of-fact tone of a linguistics professor. Very FotC in its combination of Bret's innocent error and Murray's earnest analysis.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I'm not even suppose to manage one band. I'm suppose to be working at the New Zealand Consulate, Bret.

jokes.text: So is a guitar or a keyboard? / Exactly. It's the best of both worlds. / So it starts out as a keyboard and ends kind of as a guitar. / I don't like it. It's weird. / It's not the right shape.
jokes.setup: Murray interrogates the keytar's identity.
jokes.punchline: Bret's verdict on the keytar: 'It's not the right shape.' Not 'it sounds bad,' not 'it's gimmicky' — it's that the shape is wrong. This is the most aesthetically precise and least musical objection possible.
jokes.explanation: 'It's not the right shape' as the final verdict on an instrument is very Bret — specific, slightly off, completely sincere. The objection reveals his aesthetic conservatism more than any musical argument would.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Does he play any other instruments?

dialogue.speaker: Demetri
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Come on, what? Is that what you want? I'm gonna break your keytar. I'm gonna break your keytar.

dialogue.speaker: Demetri
dialogue.line: You think I care?

jokes.text: Murray on the keytar: 'So it starts out as a keyboard and ends kind of as a guitar. / I don't like it. It's weird. / It's not the right shape.'
jokes.setup: Murray asks for clarification on the keytar's nature.
jokes.punchline: After a lengthy description of what it is, Murray's verdict is 'I don't like it. It's weird. It's not the right shape.' — aesthetics-based rejection of a real instrument that exists and has fans.
jokes.explanation: Murray's snap dismissal based on shape is funny in its arbitrariness. 'It's not the right shape' is a weirdly authoritative aesthetic judgment from someone whose band plays to almost no one.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: # The distant future, the year 2000 #

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: # Arf arf arf arf #

jokes.text: Demetri plays the keytar. Murray asks him to hold it while he listens, then: 'No, don't like it. Ugh, no. Weird.' Then: 'Oh, that bit's good.'
jokes.setup: Demetri performs on the keytar for Murray's assessment.
jokes.punchline: Murray's running critical commentary — instant negative, then qualified positive for 'that bit' — treats the audition like a dismissive A&R executive, which is the opposite of Murray's actual standing.
jokes.explanation: The joke is in Murray's oblivious authority — he's auditioning musicians for a band with no gigs, but his manner is that of a hard-to-please producer. 'Oh, that bit's good' after comprehensive rejection is a nice beat.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: It's going pretty good, except that he copies my moves.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: What moves?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: My dance moves.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You don't have dance moves.

jokes.text: Murray sees Todd and the original band arrive: 'Oh, here we go. The Fonzies have arrived.'
jokes.setup: Todd enters with the remainder of the band.
jokes.punchline: Murray's dismissal — 'The Fonzies' — is a nickname for people who think they're cool, delivered with resigned eyeroll energy. Coming from Murray, who moments ago called Todd 'the Pied Piper of cool,' this reversal is noted.
jokes.explanation: Mildly clever — 'The Fonzies' as a collective noun for cool-posturing people is functional. Murray has now switched sides, so the comment has a little dramatic irony. Not a standout joke.

dialogue.speaker: Demetri
dialogue.line: Todd and I are going to start a group... our own group without Bret and Jerome.

jokes.text: Bret calls his competing band 'Original Flight of the Conchords.' / Jemaine: 'You can't call it that.' / Bret: 'It's not. It's called the Original Flight of the Conchords. You guys can be called Flight of the Condors, or whatever.'
jokes.setup: A naming dispute breaks out between the two factions.
jokes.punchline: Bret's claim that the correct name is 'Original Flight of the Conchords' — which contains 'Flight of the Conchords' — while telling Jemaine's group to use 'Flight of the Condors, or whatever' is a spectacular piece of logic-resistant stubbornness.
jokes.explanation: The naming dispute is a rich comic vein and Bret's 'Condors, or whatever' is a great throwaway. The episode has been building a joke about band names and this is a satisfying escalation — two competing ridiculous names, both claimed with total conviction.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I'm flabbergasted.

jokes.text: Todd: 'We're gonna be the Crazy Dogggz.' / Murray: 'Shut up, Todd.' / Todd: 'Don't tell me to shut up in front of people.'
jokes.setup: Todd reveals the new band name during the dispute.
jokes.punchline: Murray's unusually sharp 'Shut up, Todd' and Todd's indignant response ('Don't tell me to shut up in front of people') escalates the tension comically — Todd is sensitive about being shut up publicly, a weirdly specific dignity claim.
jokes.explanation: The joke is in Todd's specific objection: he'd presumably be fine being told to shut up in private, but public shut-ups violate his code. Murray's brief moment of actual authority is also funny given his usual uselessness.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Hey, you got one of the Crazy Dogggz t-shirts.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: It says Crazy Dogggz on it.

jokes.text: Demetri is playing keytar in the background during the argument. Todd: 'What do you play, the leather jacket?' / Demetri: 'I play the bongos, man.'
jokes.setup: Todd dismisses Demetri during the confrontation.
jokes.punchline: Todd's insult — 'What do you play, the leather jacket?' — mocks Demetri's look, but Demetri's reply 'I play the bongos, man' is itself the punchline: Demetri doesn't play bongos (he plays keytar), which means he's either mocking Todd or is incredibly confused.
jokes.explanation: Double-layered joke: Todd's leather-jacket insult is fine, but Demetri claiming to play bongos (when he plays keytar) is the actual punchline. It's ambiguous whether this is deliberate provocation or Demetri genuinely misunderstanding — the ambiguity makes it funnier. 'The leather jacket' as an instrument is also a wonderfully absurd construction.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Hey, Mel, you didn't make it to our gig the other...

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Oh, yeah.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: There was no one there... without you there.

jokes.text: Murray: 'Why did you fire Bret, Jemaine?' / Jemaine: 'Well, I was trying to fire Todd but he did a sad face at me.'
jokes.setup: Murray demands an explanation for the Bret firing.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's full explanation for why he couldn't fire Todd and instead fired Bret is that Todd 'did a sad face at me' — one of the simplest possible emotional manipulations completely defeated Jemaine's resolve.
jokes.explanation: The episode's best single line. 'He did a sad face at me' perfectly captures Jemaine's conflict-aversion, the absurd ease of manipulating him, and the complete failure of the mission — all in seven words. It recontextualizes the entire headlock sequence retroactively (previously we were told it was a persuasive argument in the headlock; now the real mechanism is revealed as a pouting face). Exceptional economy and character integration. This is the episode's quotability champion.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Number one in 24 countries.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: They say hi?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: No.

jokes.text: Murray: 'I don't know if you can fire Bret. You're equal band members. It's got to come from a higher authority.' / Murray: 'Are you sure?' / Murray: 'Ye... no. I'll have to check, but it doesn't sound right.'
jokes.setup: Murray opines on the legality of Bret's firing.
jokes.punchline: Murray declares he's a 'higher authority,' is asked if he's sure, and immediately self-corrects to 'Ye... no. I'll have to check.' His authority is entirely theoretical and self-undermining.
jokes.explanation: Murray's collapsed authority claim is well-executed. The half-committed 'Ye... no' is a great beat — he's been walking back confidence all episode. Consistent character work.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: How giant is it?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: That's what it's called... Giant. So I'm guessing pretty big.

jokes.text: It's got to come from a higher authority. / Are you sure? / Ye... no. I'll have to check.
jokes.setup: Murray claims firing a band member requires his authorization.
jokes.punchline: When Jemaine asks if Murray's sure, Murray starts to confirm and immediately backtracks: 'Ye... no. I'll have to check.' Murray doesn't actually know his own authority, which undermines his entire claim about being the 'higher authority.'
jokes.explanation: Murray's half-second authority claim followed by genuine uncertainty is a nice self-undermining joke. 'Ye... no.' is perfectly timed.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Giant Stadium.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: How giant is it?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: That's what it's called... Giant. So I'm guessing pretty big.

jokes.text: Bret: 'What do you care for anyway, Bret? You're always quitting. Probably just quit in half an hour anyway.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine dismisses the significance of Bret's firing.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine points out that Bret has a habit of quitting, making the firing almost redundant — Bret's own pattern undermines his grievance.
jokes.explanation: Decent character moment — Bret's habit of quitting is apparently established series lore being deployed here. The specificity of 'half an hour' is funnier than a round number would be. Functions well as character continuity humor.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I've got lunch with Tori Amos.

jokes.text: Murray: 'I'm not even supposed to manage one band. I'm supposed to be working at the New Zealand Consulate, Bret.'
jokes.setup: Murray explains why he can't manage two bands.
jokes.punchline: His objection is not that two bands is too much work, but that he's already exceeding his actual job description by managing one — the consulate job being his real employment is a recurring revelation treated as new information.
jokes.explanation: The consulate job is a running series joke — Murray manages a struggling band as a side project to his actual government employment. Reminding us of this with 'I'm not even supposed to manage one band' is good series-continuity humor.

jokes.text: Murray: 'Murray, do you think you could find another cubicle?' / Murray: 'Oh, sorry, Greg. Yeah, all right.'
jokes.setup: The band meeting is apparently happening in Murray's office at the New Zealand Consulate, with his colleague Greg present.
jokes.punchline: Greg asking Murray to find another cubicle — implying the entire confrontation is happening in a government office cubicle — and Murray deferring immediately makes clear how little authority Murray actually has.
jokes.explanation: A callback to the earlier Greg interruption, now escalating to Greg exerting authority over Murray's physical space. The consulate cubicle setting for a dramatic band confrontation is an inherently funny juxtaposition.

jokes.text: [Song: 'The Distant Future'] — Robot characters sing 'The humans are dead / We used poisonous gasses / And we poisoned their asses.'
jokes.setup: A snippet of the Conchords' earlier song 'The Humans Are Dead' plays as a transitional moment (this is an existing FotC song used here as a scene interlude).
jokes.punchline: 'We used poisonous gasses / And we poisoned their asses' — a rhyme scheme that lands 'asses' as the payoff to a declaration of robot-led genocide, completely deadpan.
jokes.explanation: Even as a brief interlude, the 'gasses/asses' couplet is a genuinely funny piece of writing — apocalyptic content with a deliberately low-rent rhyme that undercuts the gravitas. The robots' clinical announcement of extinction paired with 'asses' is perfectly timed dark comedy.

jokes.text: Bret complains that Demetri copies his dance moves. / Jemaine: 'What moves? You don't have dance moves.' / Bret: 'He also started copying my look.' / Jemaine: 'What look?'
jokes.setup: Bret describes problems with his new bandmate Demetri.
jokes.punchline: Both 'what moves' and 'what look' — Jemaine's flat denials that Bret has any distinctive moves or look to copy — are devastating in their brevity. Bret apparently has a self-image that Jemaine sees no evidence for.
jokes.explanation: The parallel structure ('What moves? / What look?') makes this doubly effective — each denial is funny, and the pair together shows Jemaine systematically dismantling Bret's self-conception. The economy is exceptional: two questions that say everything. Classic FotC understatement.

jokes.text: Demetri and me are doing pretty well. It's probably better than you and me as well. / Really? / Yeah. / Hmm.
jokes.setup: The two former bandmates compare notes on their new bands.
jokes.punchline: Both Bret and Jemaine separately admit their new bands are better than Flight of the Conchords was. 'Hmm.' — Jemaine's response to learning the band he's in is probably worse than even the Original Flight of the Conchords with a keytar player — is the perfect non-reaction.
jokes.explanation: The structural symmetry — both halves of the band independently admitting their new lineups are better — is a clean comedic payoff. Jemaine's 'Hmm.' absorbs the information without drama. Deadpan at its best.

jokes.text: Murray calls the band meeting: 'Leave those girls alone there and come over.' / Jemaine: 'Okay, Bret.' / Murray: 'Jemaine.' / Jemaine: 'Standing right next to you.'
jokes.setup: Murray calls roll for the meeting, calling Bret instead of Jemaine when Jemaine is immediately beside him.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's flat 'Standing right next to you' — delivered without indignation, just as a statement of fact — is perfect. Murray can't recognize someone directly adjacent to him.
jokes.explanation: The roll-call bit from the opening returns. 'Standing right next to you' is a compact and satisfying reply that says a lot about Murray's relationship with spatial awareness and basic attention. The no-drama delivery from Jemaine is key.

jokes.text: Went really good tonight. Really good, you two. / Thank you. / And good, you two too.
jokes.setup: Murray is praising both bands after their gig
jokes.punchline: 'And good, you two too' — Murray's attempt to divide praise equally results in increasingly awkward phrasing
jokes.explanation: The sonic awkwardness of 'you two too' is a mild wordplay beat. Functional but not particularly strong.

jokes.text: Murray: 'Actually, Jemaine, what about that song of Todd's? You could do the one about the dogs. "The Doggy Bounce."' / Jemaine: 'Bring that one in. I like that one.' [Todd]: 'Arf arf.' / Jemaine: 'Yeah, it's not ready though.'
jokes.setup: Murray suggests performing Todd's song.
jokes.punchline: 'Yeah, it's not ready though' — Jemaine deploys his standby excuse again, despite the song having been performed and sung multiple times throughout the episode.
jokes.explanation: A clean callback — 'it's not ready' having been deployed once before and now returned as an established Jemaine avoidance mechanism. The episode has beaten 'The Doggy Bounce' into the audience's brain for 20 minutes, so 'it's not ready' is a deeply satisfying piece of comic stubbornness.

jokes.text: Jemaine and Todd argue: 'It's been ready! It's been ready!' — Murray: 'Come on, listen. Look, shake hands.' / 'He's squeezing my hand a little bit.' / Todd: 'Are you? Just my regular handshake.' / Bret: 'There's no squeeze there. What a wuss.'
jokes.setup: Murray forces a handshake between Jemaine and Todd to resolve their argument.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine complains of a squeeze; Todd denies it; Bret calls Jemaine a wuss — the power dynamics of the handshake become a whole new arena of conflict.
jokes.explanation: The handshake-squeeze micro-conflict is a good escalation beat — what should be a peace gesture becomes another confrontation. Bret's 'What a wuss' from the sideline is a small but effective kicker.

jokes.text: He's squeezing my hand a little bit. / Are you? / Just my regular handshake. / There's no squeeze there. What a wuss.
jokes.setup: Murray makes the two bands shake hands to make peace.
jokes.punchline: Todd is subtly crushing Bret's hand during the reconciliatory handshake. When called out, he denies it and insults Bret: 'What a wuss.' Todd's dominance assertion through handshake grip is both petty and in-character.
jokes.explanation: Classic alpha-male handshake bit. Not especially original but Todd's commitment to the denial and the insult is character-specific.

jokes.text: I'm flabbergasted.
jokes.setup: Todd delivers a sincere, heartfelt goodbye to Murray before departing with Demetri.
jokes.punchline: Murray's response to Todd's touching farewell is the single word 'I'm flabbergasted.' The vocabulary choice ('flabbergasted') is exactly Murray — slightly formal, slightly outdated, delivered with complete sincerity.
jokes.explanation: 'I'm flabbergasted' as the emotional climax of a band breakup is perfectly Murray. The word choice is funny because it's sincere — this is genuinely how Murray would describe his emotional state. Economy is exceptional: two words carry all the comedy.

jokes.text: 'Doggy dos and doggy don'ts / Doggy wills and doggy won'ts / Arf arf, do the doggy bounce' — sustained lyric section
jokes.setup: The Doggy Bounce continues with its lyrical content.
jokes.punchline: 'Doggy don'ts / Doggy won'ts' as rhyming constructs — the grammatical inversion of 'dos and don'ts' into 'wills and won'ts' is a neat internal rhyme scheme that both mocks and kind of nails pop-song formula.
jokes.explanation: Solid wordplay within the song — 'wills and won'ts' as a counterpart to 'dos and don'ts' is a tidy construction. More impressive on reflection than in the moment. Scores modestly because it's embedded in a song being satirized, so the wordplay is the joke within the joke.

jokes.text: Mel is wearing a Crazy Dogggz t-shirt. Bret: 'Hey, you got one of the Crazy Dogggz t-shirts.' / Mel: 'No.'
jokes.setup: The Conchords encounter Mel after the gig.
jokes.punchline: Mel is visibly wearing a Crazy Dogggz t-shirt but denies it — she is protecting the Conchords' feelings by lying while wearing the evidence of her defection.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest jokes. 'No.' as a one-word denial while wearing the shirt is a masterclass in FotC deadpan. The visual gag (shirt) + the denial = a perfect economy. It encapsulates Mel's character (obsessive fan who can't admit divided loyalties), the show's understatement style, and the episode's Todd arc all in one beat. Exceptionally high rewatch bonus.

jokes.text: Hey, Mel, you didn't make it to our gig the other... / Oh, yeah. / There was no one there... [long pause] without you there.
jokes.setup: Bret starts to tell Mel she missed their gig. He trails off, then recalibrates.
jokes.punchline: Bret starts to say 'There was no one there' — implying the gig was completely empty — but catches himself and adds 'without you there.' The long pause between 'no one there' and the qualifier reveals he almost admitted the gig had no audience. The recovery makes it worse.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully structured cringe moment. The pause between 'There was no one there...' and '...without you there' is everything — Bret catching himself mid-confession, trying to make it romantic, and making it more pathetic in the process. This is Bret's character perfectly captured: small, hopeful, inadvertently revealing. The transcript pause [24:03] confirms the timing is deliberate. Justifies 8+ based on structure and character integration.

jokes.text: Mel rushes off; Jemaine plaintively calls after her: 'Hey, Mel, you didn't make it to our gig the other...' (trailing off as she leaves) / '...There was no one there...' / '...without you there.'
jokes.setup: Mel rushes away and the Conchords try to call after her.
jokes.punchline: The trailing revelation that Mel is apparently their ONLY audience member — 'There was no one there... without you there' — delivered with quiet devastation is both funny and genuinely sad. The pauses between lines create a perfect comedic/tragic rhythm.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed tragicomic moment. The revelation unfolds across three beats, each more devastating: 'you didn't make it' → 'there was no one there' → 'without you there.' It's both the episode's emotional nadir and one of its funniest moments, which is exactly the FotC sweet spot. The pauses as Mel walks away allow the full weight to land.

jokes.text: Murray returns after an apparent long absence: 'Well, sorry I haven't been in touch. I've just been so busy, man, you know?' — then lists the Crazy Dogggz' achievements: 'Number one in 24 countries. / We did one gig... 98,000 people. That was in Rio. / The President of Peru invited us to like a dinner. We had parfaits. / I got like a big sun lounger.'
jokes.setup: Murray checks in after apparently touring South America with the Crazy Dogggz.
jokes.punchline: The escalating list of achievements (24 countries, Rio, President of Peru) ends with Murray's personal highlight: 'I got like a big sun lounger.' The deflation from 'President of Peru' to 'sun lounger' as equal-weight achievements is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The escalation structure is excellent: each item is more impressive than the last until 'I got like a big sun lounger' lands as Murray's equal entry. 'We had parfaits' is also an unexpectedly specific and funny detail — fine dining with a head of state and Murray clocks the dessert. The 'like a big sun lounger' hedge ('like') is a perfect Murray verbal tic.

jokes.text: Well, sorry I haven't been in touch. I've just been so busy, man, you know? / How are the Crazy Dogggz? / They're a phenomenon. Number one in 24 countries. / They say hi? / No.
jokes.setup: Murray returns after a long absence. Bret and Jemaine ask about the Crazy Dogggz.
jokes.punchline: 'They say hi? / No.' Murray has been managing the Crazy Dogggz to global success and the band doesn't even acknowledge Bret and Jemaine's existence. The casualness of 'No' — no apology, no softening — is the joke.
jokes.explanation: 'They say hi? / No.' is a brutal three-word exchange. The flatness of 'No' — Murray doesn't even try to cushion it — is perfectly Murray and perfectly funny. Economy score of 9 is warranted.

jokes.text: Know the dog? It was my idea. / Really? / It's Toby.
jokes.setup: Murray describes his creative contributions to the Crazy Dogggz music video.
jokes.punchline: Murray's proudest contribution to the music video is 'the dog' — and specifically that the dog is named Toby. He knows the dog's name. The fact that using a dog named Toby is Murray's claimed directorial creative vision is wonderfully pathetic.
jokes.explanation: 'It's Toby' as the final word on Murray's directorial contribution — the dog's name — is a perfect deadpan capper. Murray's pride in knowing the dog's name implies this is the level of detail he engaged with on the video. Highly character-integrated.

jokes.text: We did one gig... 98,000 people. That was in Rio. Then the President of Peru invited us to like a dinner. We had parfaits. I got like a big sun lounger.
jokes.setup: Murray describes the Crazy Dogggz's South American tour.
jokes.punchline: The escalation goes: Rio gig → Presidential dinner → parfaits → sun lounger. Each item is less impressive than the one before, reaching its nadir with Murray's primary takeaway from an invitation by a Head of State: he got a sun lounger.
jokes.explanation: The de-escalating list is very well-structured: Presidential dinner is impressive, parfaits is amusing, sun lounger is the perfect comic nadir. Murray's sun lounger being the highlight of a South American presidential state dinner is a brilliant piece of character comedy. The escalation-as-de-escalation structure is original.

jokes.text: Murray: 'Do we have any gigs, Murray?' / Murray: 'Yeah. Yeah, you've got the library. They want you back for the bingo night.'
jokes.setup: After hearing about the Crazy Dogggz' global success, the Conchords ask about their own bookings.
jokes.punchline: Bingo night at the library. Following the story of 98,000 people in Rio and presidential dinners, the contrast is the entire joke.
jokes.explanation: The joke is entirely structural — the juxtaposition of global pop success and library bingo night does all the work. 'They want you back' implies they've played library bingo before and it went well enough to return, which adds a layer. The economy is excellent; Murray says it as if it's equivalent to the Crazy Dogggz' stadium.

jokes.text: Murray: 'Actually that's the same night that the Crazy Dogggz have got a big gig. Giant Stadium. / How giant is it? / That's what it's called... Giant. So I'm guessing pretty big.'
jokes.setup: Bret asks about the Crazy Dogggz' venue.
jokes.punchline: Murray has booked the Conchords for library bingo on the same night the Crazy Dogggz play 'Giant Stadium' — and when asked how big it is, he clarifies the name IS 'Giant' and applies the name literally to estimate the size.
jokes.explanation: 'That's what it's called... Giant. So I'm guessing pretty big' is a lovely piece of deadpan logic — Murray applies the proper noun literally as evidence of size. The joke escalates the library/stadium contrast while adding a wordplay layer.

jokes.text: Murray: 'So I'm gonna go there first, set them up, and then come back to the library, help you guys out, and then probably to and fro between the library and the stadium.' / Bret: 'Yeah, Murray, it seems like you're spending a lot more time with the Crazy Dogggz than us.' / Murray: 'No, equal time. Check my schedules, it's equal.'
jokes.setup: Murray describes his plan for the night of the competing gigs.
jokes.punchline: Murray's plan involves going to the stadium, coming back to the library, and then going 'to and fro' — this is clearly not equal time — and his defense is 'Check my schedules, it's equal,' delivered with complete confidence.
jokes.explanation: Murray's fairness delusion is a good character beat. 'Check my schedules' — plural — implies an elaborate system of documentation for what is obviously unfair treatment. The confidence of his denial undercuts his supposed managerial competence.

jokes.text: So I'm gonna go there first, set them up, and then come back to the library, help you guys out, and then probably to and fro between the library and the stadium.
jokes.setup: Murray explains his scheduling plan for the night of conflicting gigs.
jokes.punchline: Murray plans to shuttle between a 98,000-capacity stadium and a library bingo night, apparently finding this a perfectly equitable arrangement. 'To and fro between the library and the stadium' as if these are equivalent venues.
jokes.explanation: Functional joke about Murray's false equivalence between the two gig venues. Sets up the next joke well.

jokes.text: Murray: 'I can't discuss it now 'cause I've got to go. I've got lunch with Tori Amos.'
jokes.setup: Murray cuts off the conversation about equal time with the Conchords.
jokes.punchline: His reason for leaving is that he has a lunch date with Tori Amos — an implausible celebrity that is casually dropped with complete sincerity, as if this is a normal part of Murray's schedule.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'Tori Amos' — not a major pop star, not someone obviously connected to bongo music, just a specific and somehow perfect choice — is the whole joke. Murray's 'I've got lunch with Tori Amos' as a conversation-ender, delivered without explanation or context, is classic FotC absurdist name-dropping.

tmdb_overview: Murray introduces a new bongo-playing band member on Bret and Jemaine, triggering angry dances and mutant half-bands.
